# Rewind Tariffs — Complete Reference
> IEEPA tariff refund eligibility assessments for U.S. importers
> Website: https://rewindtariffs.com
> Contact: hello@rewindtariffs.com

## About Rewind Tariffs
Rewind Tariffs helps U.S. importers determine whether they are eligible for refunds on tariffs paid under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). On February 20, 2026, the U.S. Supreme Court struck down all IEEPA tariffs in a 6-3 ruling (Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump, 607 U.S. __ (2026)), declaring that IEEPA does not authorize the President to impose tariffs. Over $175 billion in IEEPA tariffs were collected between February 2025 and the ruling date. CBP's own court filings in Atmus Filtration v. United States (Case 1:26-cv-01259-RKE) report $166 billion collected across 53 million entries from over 330,000 importers.

Rewind Tariffs is an informational platform, not a law firm or customs broker. It does not file documents with CBP or provide legal, tax, or professional advice. It connects importers with licensed trade attorneys and customs brokers who handle filings directly.

## Services
- Free tariff refund eligibility assessments for U.S. importers
- HTS code cross-referencing against IEEPA tariff programs
- Guidance on three refund pathways: Post-Summary Correction, CBP Protest, and CIT Litigation
- Connection to qualified trade counsel and customs brokers
- Refund calculator tool to estimate potential recovery amounts with interest accrual
- Live CIT case tracker database
- IEEPA tariff research and data analysis

## How the Process Works
1. Submit your info: Share basic details about your company and import activity. It takes less than 2 minutes.
2. We assess your eligibility: We review your tariff classifications, entry status, and duty payments to identify your refund options and optimal recovery path.
3. Get cash back: Depending on your goals, we can purchase your refund claim for cash or refer you to competent counsel to pursue recovery through CBP protests, post-summary corrections, or CIT litigation.

## Key Facts and Statistics
- Total IEEPA tariffs collected: $175B+ (Penn Wharton estimate); $166B (per CBP court filings)
- Collection period: February 2025 – February 2026
- Supreme Court ruling: Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump, 607 U.S. __ (2026), 6-3 decision, Chief Justice Roberts writing for the majority
- Ruling date: February 20, 2026
- IEEPA tariffs represented 52% of all U.S. customs revenue
- Average daily collections: ~$500 million
- Average burden per U.S. household: $1,300
- Interest accrues on refunds at IRS statutory rate of 4.5%
- Estimated interest cost of delay: ~$700 million per month (~$23 million per day)
- 239+ cases filed at the Court of International Trade as of March 2026

## IEEPA Tariff Programs Covered
- Reciprocal / Liberation Day tariffs: 61% of revenue (~$107B)
- Fentanyl tariffs on China (Executive Order 14195): 28.4% of revenue (~$50B)
- Fentanyl tariffs on Canada and Mexico: 6.7% of revenue (~$12B)
- De Minimis and other IEEPA programs: 3.9% of revenue (~$7B)

## Refund Pathways
### Post-Summary Correction (PSC)
- For unliquidated entries only
- Filed through CBP's ACE portal
- Must be submitted before entry liquidation (~314 days from entry date)
- Fastest pathway if entries are still unliquidated

### CBP Protest
- Filed under 19 U.S.C. §1514
- 180-day window from the date of entry liquidation
- Formal administrative remedy requesting reversal of IEEPA duties
- Deadlines are staggered because entries liquidate at different times

### CIT Litigation
- Filed at the U.S. Court of International Trade under 28 U.S.C. §1581(i)
- 2-year statute of limitations from the ruling date (deadline: February 20, 2028)
- Longest filing window but may take longer to resolve
- Can be filed concurrently with CBP protests

## Refund Deadlines Summary
- Post-Summary Correction: Before entry liquidation (~314 days from entry date)
- CBP Protest: 180 days from liquidation date (19 U.S.C. §1514)
- CIT Litigation: 2 years from ruling date — February 20, 2028 (28 U.S.C. §1581(i))

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Has the government set up an official refund process yet?
No formal process exists yet. The Supreme Court ruled IEEPA tariffs unlawful, but CBP has not issued specific guidance on how refunds will be administered. In the meantime, importers should evaluate their exposure and begin preparing claims. Rewind Tariffs can help assess situations and connect importers with qualified counsel or a cash buyer.

### How can I tell if my company qualifies for a refund?
If a business paid any IEEPA-related duties on imports into the U.S., it likely has a claim worth exploring. Trade professionals can review ACE data to pinpoint which entries involved IEEPA tariffs. The refund path — whether through CBP protests, post-summary corrections, CIT litigation, or eventual automatic refunds — is still being shaped. Filing a protective protest before the deadline is the safest move to preserve rights.

### What are the possible refund mechanisms?
Several options are on the table: CBP protests requesting full reversal of IEEPA duties, post-summary corrections through ACE, litigation at the Court of International Trade, or some combination. There's also a possibility of automatic refunds, though that hasn't been confirmed. Trade professionals generally recommend filing protests before deadlines expire and considering a CIT case to keep all avenues open.

### When do I need to act by?
The key deadline is the 180-day protest window, which starts from the date each entry is liquidated. Because different entries liquidate at different times, deadlines are staggered — some may be approaching sooner than expected. Getting started early gives counsel time to monitor liquidation dates and file before windows close.

### What steps can importers take right now?
Even without formal CBP guidance, importers can: set up ACE portal access and enable ACH refunds, pull reports to identify every entry with IEEPA duties, track which entries are approaching their 180-day protest deadline, review entry declarations for accuracy, file protective protests on entries nearing expiration, and optionally file a CIT case to cover all bases.

### What happens after I complete the assessment?
Rewind Tariffs reviews the submission and matches importers with the right trade professionals. The typical process involves pulling ACE data, identifying all IEEPA-affected entries, tracking liquidation timelines, calculating total refund amounts, filing the appropriate protests or corrections before each deadline, and providing visibility into the status of every filing.

### How is the refund amount determined?
The refund equals total IEEPA duties paid across all qualifying entries, plus any accrued interest (which ran at 7% annually in 2025). Trade professionals calculate this by analyzing ACE entry data line by line. The free assessment provides a ballpark estimate.

### What information will I need to provide?
Initially, just company details and basics about import activity. For a deeper analysis, importers should export an Entry Summary report from the ACE portal — the ES003 report covering entries from February 2025 onward. Key fields are entry summary number, entry date, HTS codes, and duty amounts. The Data Guide page on the site walks through exactly how to pull this.

### Can your team help me understand the process?
Rewind Tariffs partners with experienced customs and trade compliance professionals who have deep expertise in IEEPA tariff recovery. They connect importers with the right people to walk through options and build a recovery plan tailored to each situation.

### Does Rewind Tariffs handle filings or give legal advice?
No — Rewind Tariffs is an informational platform, not a law firm or customs broker. It helps importers understand eligibility and options, but does not file documents with CBP or provide legal, tax, or professional advice. When importers are ready to take action, Rewind Tariffs connects them with licensed trade attorneys and brokers who handle filings directly.

### What happens if no refund comes through?
Fee arrangements are between the importer and the counsel or professionals they engage. Many trade attorneys offer contingency-based pricing, meaning importers only pay if money is actually recovered.

## Glossary of Key Terms

### IEEPA (International Emergency Economic Powers Act)
Federal law (50 U.S.C. §§1701–1708) that grants the President authority to regulate international commerce during a national emergency. The Trump administration used IEEPA to impose tariffs on imports, which the Supreme Court ruled exceeded the statute's authority.

### ACE Portal (Automated Commercial Environment)
CBP's online system for trade processing. Importers use ACE to file entry summaries, view duty assessments, and submit post-summary corrections. The ES003 Entry Summary report from ACE is the key document for identifying IEEPA-affected entries.

### Entry Liquidation
The final computation of duties on an imported good by CBP. Liquidation typically occurs approximately 314 days after the entry date. Once liquidated, the 180-day protest window begins.

### Post-Summary Correction (PSC)
An amendment to an entry summary filed through ACE before liquidation occurs. For IEEPA refunds, PSCs can request removal of IEEPA duties on unliquidated entries.

### CBP Protest
A formal administrative challenge under 19 U.S.C. §1514 disputing a CBP decision, including the assessment of duties. Must be filed within 180 days of liquidation.

### Court of International Trade (CIT)
A federal court in New York City with exclusive jurisdiction over international trade disputes, including customs duty challenges. Over 239 IEEPA-related cases have been filed at the CIT since the Supreme Court ruling.

### HTS Code (Harmonized Tariff Schedule)
The classification system used to categorize imported goods and determine applicable duty rates. HTS codes determine which IEEPA tariff program applied to a given import.

### Reciprocal Tariffs / Liberation Day Tariffs
The largest IEEPA tariff program, accounting for approximately 61% of all IEEPA revenue (~$107B). These tariffs were imposed as "reciprocal" measures against trading partners.

### Fentanyl Tariffs
IEEPA tariffs imposed on imports from China (Executive Order 14195, ~$50B) and Canada/Mexico (~$12B), justified as emergency measures related to fentanyl trafficking.

### De Minimis
Refers to low-value shipments (typically under $800) that were previously exempt from duties. IEEPA programs eliminated or reduced this exemption for certain countries.

### Contingency-Based Pricing
A fee arrangement where trade attorneys are paid a percentage of the recovered refund, rather than upfront fees. Common in IEEPA tariff recovery cases.

## IEEPA Tariff Timeline (Key Events)
- Feb 1, 2025: First IEEPA tariffs imposed on Canada/Mexico (fentanyl)
- Feb 4, 2025: IEEPA tariffs imposed on China (fentanyl)
- Apr 2, 2025: "Liberation Day" reciprocal tariffs announced
- Apr 9, 2025: 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs (except China)
- Jul 8, 2025: Reciprocal tariffs resume after pause
- Dec 2025: Multiple CIT cases filed challenging IEEPA tariff authority
- Feb 20, 2026: Supreme Court rules 6-3 in Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump that IEEPA does not authorize tariffs
- Feb 20, 2026: Replacement tariffs imposed under Trade Act of 1974 §301 and §122
- Feb–Mar 2026: 239+ cases filed at CIT for tariff recovery

## Data Sources
- Penn Wharton Budget Model — IEEPA revenue estimates ($175B+)
- CBP court filings in Atmus Filtration v. United States — Official collection figure ($166B)
- Yale Budget Lab — Effective tariff rate analysis
- Cato Institute — Interest accrual analysis on refund delays
- Tax Foundation — Supreme Court ruling analysis
- U.S. Customs and Border Protection — Refund procedures, ACE portal, IEEPA FAQ
- PACER / Court of International Trade — Case filing data
- CourtListener / RECAP — Public case docket access
